2 PRELIMINARY STATEMENT
23 1. Jessie Evans, the former head men’s basketball coach at the University of San
24 || Francisco (USF), brings this action for damages for breach of contract, breach of the covenant of
25 good faith and fair dealing, interference with contractual rights, and interference with prospective
6 . .
2 economic advantage, based upon USF’s unlawful termination of his employment contract and related

1 | actions. Although Evans’ contract provides for his termination without cause and for USF’s payment
21 of liquidated damages upon such occurrence, defendant USF falsely claimed that it had just cause to
terminate his contract in order to avoid making payments required by his contract. Additionally,
4
defendant USF made false statements about plaintiff's job performance and alleged violations of
5
P National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) rules, thereby interfering with his ability to obtain
7 || new employment as a college basketball coach.
8 JURISDICTION AND VENUE
9 2. Plaintiff Evans’ claims arise under the statutory and common law of the State of
10 California.
11
3. The actions and events giving rise to this lawsuit occurred in the City and County of

San Francisco.

14 PARTIES
15 4. At times relevant hereto, plaintiff JESSIE EVANS was the Head Basketball Coach of

17 5. At all times relevant hereto, defendant UNIVERSITY OF SAN FRANCISCO was and

San Francisco.

oD) DOES 1 through 15, inclusive, and therefore sues said defendants by such fictitious names. Plaintiff

Evans v. The University of San Francisco, No. CGC 08-473864
First Amended Complaint - 2

 
1 | Plaintiff is informed and believes, and thereon alleges, that DOES 1 through 15 are residents of the
2 | State of California.
; STATEMENT OF FACTS
7. Jessie Evans is a well-respected college basketball coach who served as an assistant
6 coach for NCAA Division 1 men’s basketball programs at University of Minnesota, San Diego State,
7 University of Wyoming, University of Texas, and University of Arizona from 1976 through 1997.
8 8. Evans became the head coach at the University of Louisiana-Lafayette in 1997 and led
9 || his team to the NCAA Tournament in 2000 and 2003. Evans left the University of Louisiana-
10 Lafayette voluntarily to accept the position of head men’s basketball coach at USF. At the time he
M left the University of Louisiana-Lafayette Evans was in good standing and had good cause to believe
that he could have remained in that position for as long as he wished.
14 9. In 2004, USF Executive Director of Athletics Bill Hogan recruited Evans to be the
15 | Head Basketball Coach at USF. In dosing so he falsely assured Evans that he could look forward to a
16 | long career at USF and that USF would scrupulously observe all terms of its Employment Agreement
17 || with Evans, including the five-year initial period of employment set forth therein.
18 10. In light of USF’s representations and in reasonable reliance upon its assurances that it
” would honor its contractual commitments to him, Evans resigned from his position at the University
. of Louisiana-Lafayette, in Lafayette, Louisiana, and relocated to San Francisco with his wife.
oD) 11. On April 21, 2004, USF and Evans entered into an Employment Agreement whereby
93 | Evans would serve as the Head Coach of USF’s men’s intercollegiate basketball team for five years,
24 || through April 20, 2009. USF agreed to pay Evans a base salary of $200,000 per year, plus (a) annual
25 || increases to be determined by the Executive Director of Athletics; (b) standard USF fringe benefits,
26 including health insurance, life insurance, disability, vacation, and retirement plans; (c) housing
27
| Evans wThe University of San Francisco,No.CGC 08473864. =O y of San Francisco, No. CGC 08-473864
First Amended Complaint - 3

 
1 || assistance and moving expenses; (d) an automobile, including liability and comprehensive insurance;
2 (e) reimbursement for out of pocket expenses; (f) term life insurance in the amount of one million

3 dollars; (g) bonuses for selection to the NCAA tournament and for each victory in that tournament;
(h) revenue sharing for men’s basketball season ticket sales in excess of 800; (i) opportunities to earn
6 outside income; (j) a guarantee of compensation for radio and television appearances of at least

7 $25,000 annually; and (k) use of USF’s facilities without charge for summer basketball camps and

g || clinics. The total annual value of USF’s contract with Evans is over $300,000.

9 12. Article 8 of the Employment Agreement between USF and Evans provides for USF’s
16 right to terminate the agreement under “certain limited circumstances.”
7 13. Article 8(b) of the Employment Agreement provides that “just cause” for the
termination of Evans’ employment means:

14 (a) “Deliberate and serious violations of Coach’s duties or refusal or unwillingness to
15 || perform such duties in good faith and to the best of Coach’s ability;

16 (b) “Material violations by Coach of any of the other terms and conditions of this

17|| Agreement not remedied after thirty (30) days written notice thereof to Coach;

18 (c) “Coach’s conviction or admission (including a plea of nolo contendere) of any

” criminal statute (excluding vehicular misdemeanors), Coach’s confirmed act of moral turpitude,
and/or a confirmed violation by Coach of any rule, regulation, constitutional provision, or bylaw of
yy) the NCAA which occurred during the term of this Agreement or prior hereto during any employment
3 | by Coach at another NCAA member institution, any of which, in the sole judgment of University
24 || would reflect adversely upon the University or its athletic program,

25 (d) “A serious and intentional violation by Coach of any law, rule, regulation,

26 constitutional provision, or bylaw of the University, the WCC or the NCAA, which in the sole

27

Evans v. The University of San Francisco, No.CGC 08473864. 2ssts—S y of San Francisco, No. CGC 08-473864
First Amended Complaint - 4

 
1 | judgment of University would reflect adversely upon the University or its athletic program, including
2 any serious violations which may result in the University being placed on probation by the WCC or

3 the NCAA and including any violation which may have occurred during prior employment by Coach
at another NCAA institution;

6 (e) “A serious and intentional violation of any material law, rule, regulation,

7 constitutional provision, or bylaw of the University, the WCC or the NCAA by a member of the

g || coaching staffer (sic) any other person under Coach’s supervision and direction, including student-

9 || athletes in the intercollegiate men’s basketball program which violation(s) are known to Coach, or
10 should have been known to Coach, and Coach fails to report said violation(s) to the University and, in
" turn said failure of Coach may, in the sole but reasonable judgment of University, reflect adversely
. upon the University or its athletic program; or
14 (f) “Prolonged unexcused absence from duty.”
15 14. Termination of its Employment Agreement with Evans for cause relieves USF of its
16 || further obligations to provide him with compensation and related benefits.

17 15.  Atno time during Evans’ employment at USF did he commit acts that would justify
18 his termination “for cause” under his Employment Agreement with USF.

” 16. Article 11 of the Employment Agreement provides that, “University shall have the
right to terminate this Agreement prior to its scheduled expiration on April 20, 2009, without cause.”
Dy In such event, “Coach shall be entitled to liquidated damages” pursuant to Article 12, which provides
93 || that USF shall pay Coach “an amount equal to Coach’s base salary due under Section 7(a) of this

24 || Agreement, as increased under Section 7(b), through the scheduled expiration date of this Agreement,
251 as may be amended from time to time in accordance with the terms hereof.”

26

27

First Amended Complaint - 5

 
. 1 17. During Evans’ first season at USF, his team compiled a record of 17 wins, 14 losses,

2 || and was selected to the National Invitation Tournament.

3 18. Evans’ career record as a head coach at USF and at Louisiana-Lafayette through the
2006-07 season was 159 wins, 129 losses (.552).

6 19. | On May 31, 2005, in recognition of Evans’ “exemplary” performance of his duties and
7 his “success with the men’s basketball team,” USF extended his Employment Agreement to April 20,
g || 2010, and increased his compensation by amending the provision on Season Ticket Revenue Sharing
9 || to state the following: “In addition to Coach’s annual base salary, University agrees to pay Coach
19 twenty five percent (25%) of all revenue on season ticket sales in excess of the 2003-04 season
M" amount ($110,000).”
. 20. On May 15, 2006, USF Executive Athletic Director Bill Hogan evaluated Evans’ job
14 performance. Hogan graded Evans as “Outstanding” or “Exceeds Expectations” on 23 out of 35
15 | performance categories and gave him no “Unsatisfactory” ratings.

16 21. On December 26, 2007, the day after Christmas, USF Executive Athletic Director

17 | Debra Gore-Mann presented Evans with an ultimatum: either (1) “request a leave of absence for the
18 remainder of season to handle a pressing personal or family issue, or other reason;” or (2) suffer an
” immediate, mid-season suspension from employment, and the commencement of proceedings to
terminate his employment “for just cause.”

09 22. Faced with this coercive and unacceptable choice between falsely stating that he had
93 || requested a leave of absence or facing the humiliating and career damaging prospect of a public

24 || suspension from his duties in the midst of the basketball season, and following discussions between
25 | his agents and USF Attorney Michael Vartain, Evans reluctantly agreed to issuance of the statement:
26

27

Evans v. The University of San Francisco,No.CGC08-473864
First Amended Complaint - 6

 
1 | “Coach Jessie Evans has requested a leave for the remainder of the basketball season.” This statement
2 I is not true.

3 23. On December 26, 2007, Gore-Mann publicly announced in a USF press release to the
San Francisco Chronicle and other media that Evans had requested a leave from his duties as head

6 coach of the USF men’s basketball program. This statement was false and misleading in that it

7 suggested that Evans had done so voluntarily rather than as a result of coercion.

8 24.  Gore-Mann’s statements damaged Evans’ reputation and harmed him in his career as a

9 || college basketball coach because they implied that he either suffered from a severe health problem
10 that interfered with his ability to function as a collegiate basketball coach or that he had committed
I serious acts of misconduct that rendered him unfit for employment as a collegiate basketball coach.
. Gore-Mann compounded the harm she inflicted on Evans by publicly claiming that he had engaged in
14 misconduct at his previous place of employment. That statement was false as well.
15 25. On information and belief, Does 9 through 13 urged Gore-Mann to terminate Evans

16 || from his position as head basketball coach at USF and to replace him with Eddie Sutton. Does 9

17]| through 13 acted in intentional disregard of Evans’ contractual agreement with USF in order to

18 induce a breach of that contract. Their statements were a substantial cause of defendant USF’s actions
” in removing Evans from his position as head basketball coach.

26. On December 26, 2007, USF and Gore-Mann announced that Eddie Sutton would

Dy) replace Evans as USF’s head basketball coach for the remainder of the 2007-08 season.

23 27. On or about March 10, 2008, USF Attorney Michael Vartain publicly stated in an

24 || article published in the San Francisco Chronicle that Evans was guilty of numerous “secondary”

23 | NCAA violations amounting to the “loss of control” of the men’s basketball program at USF.

26

27

Evans v. The University of San Francisco, No.CGC08-473864 ——iiiti‘éSOSCSC*~*™S
First Amended Complaint - 7

 
1 28. Vartain’s statements were false and severely damaged Evans’ reputation and his career
2! asa head college basketball coach. The statement that Evans had lost control of USF’s men’s
3 basketball program severely damaged his reputation in the collegiate basketball community and
effectively made it impossible for him to obtain employment as an NCAA Division 1 head basketball
6 coach or assistant basketball coach.
7 29. On March 17, 2008, Gore-Mann advised Evans that USF was terminating his
g || employment for “just cause.”
9 FIRST CLAIM FOR RELIEF — BREACH OF CONTRACT
10 (Against defendant University of San Francisco)
1 30. Plaintiff refers to and incorporates by reference paragraphs 1-29 above as though fully
12 || set forth herein.
13 31. By virtue of the foregoing, USF violated plaintiff Evans’ rights under his Employment
M4 Agreement with USF by, among other things, terminating his employment without just cause and by
'° failing to compensate him as required by the Agreement in the event of its termination without just
cause.
17
18 SECOND CLAIM FOR RELIEF — BREACH OF THE COVENANT OF GOOD FAITH
AND FAIR DEALING
19 (Against defendant University of San Francisco)
20 32. Plaintiff refers to and incorporates by reference paragraphs 1-31 above as though fully
211 set forth herein.
22 33. By virtue of the foregoing, USF violated the covenant of good faith and fair dealing in
°° its Employment Agreement with Evans through the actions of its agents, Gore-Mann, Vartain, and
, Does | through 4, in coercing Evans to state that he had requested a leave of absence, making the
OY public statements described above, and colluding with Does 9 through 13 to terminate Evans’
27 || employment.

5 (Dismissed by Court Order dated January 21, 2010)
3 FOURTH CLAIM FOR RELIEF — INTERFERENCE WITH CONTRACT AND WITH
PROSPECTIVE ECONOMIC ADVANTAGE
4 (Against defendants Does 9 through 13)
5 34. Plaintiff refers to and incorporates by reference paragraphs 1-33 above as though fully
© set forth herein.
7
35. By virtue of the foregoing, Does 9 through 13 wrongfully interfered with Evans’ rights
8
under his Employment Agreement with USF and with his rights and abilities to receive the economic
9
10 advantages of that Agreement and of future employment opportunities. Evans was damaged by the
11 | actions of Does 9-13 in interfering with his contractual rights at USF.
12 FIFTH CLAIM FOR RELIEF — DEFAMATION
(Stricken by Court Order dated July 30, 2008)
13

15
DAMAGES
16
7 36.  Asaresult of the actions of defendants, Evans has been injured and has suffered
18 damages as follows:
19 a. He has been deprived of compensation and other economic benefits to which
20 he is entitled under his Employment Agreement with USF;
21 b. He is likely to lose compensation to which he would otherwise be entitled in
22 the future;
23 eyes
c. He has suffered from emotional distress, embarrassment and humiliation;
24
95 d. His reputation in the collegiate basketball community has been damaged; and
% e. His prospects for future employment and career advancement have been
7 diminished.
a
Evans v. The University of San Francisco, No. CGC 08-473864
First Amended Complaint - 9

 
1 PUNITIVE DAMAGES
2 37. In taking the actions described above, defendants Does 9 through 13 acted
3 maliciously, oppressively, and with the intent to harm Evans and to injure him in his employment and
reputation. The actions of Does 9 through 13 in interfering with Evans’ employment were taken
6 without regard to Evans’ rights in order to harm him in his career.
7
8 WHEREFORE, plaintiff JESSIE EVANS requests that this Court grant him relief as follows:
9 (1) Damages for past and future lost wages, earnings, and benefits according to proof, against
10 all defendants;
M (2) Interest on damages at the prevailing legal rate, against all defendants;
(3) Compensatory damages for humiliation, mental anguish, emotional distress, and damage
14 to reputation, against defendants Does 9 through 13;
15 (4) Punitive damages according to proof, against defendants Does 9 through 13;
16 (5) Attorneys’ fees, against defendants Does 9 through 13;
17 (6) Costs of suit; and
18 (7) Such other and further relief as the Court may deem proper.
